inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
You want to restart running after a messy few months and cautiously rebuild toward a spring half — I hear you and that’s the right priority: steady, safe progress. Before I write your first week of training I need two quick details so the plan matches your life and risk profile:
- Your weekly time availability: how many days you can realistically train and typical session length (e.g., 4 days/week, 45–60 minutes each).
- Your current injury status: any pain or recent injuries, whether you’re currently running pain-free, and if a clinician has cleared you to resume training.
Send those two details and I’ll put together a cautious, specific first week and explain the reasoning so you know why we’re starting where we are. I’ll also include what to report after sessions so I can adjust the plan safely.

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
You said you’re an intermediate, can realistically train four days a week, and you get mild Achilles tightness when you push—good context. Below is a cautious, structured first week that protects two anchors (one easy aerobic and one strength/mobility), keeps load minimal, and gives a small, controlled tempo stimulus to start rebuilding fitness.
Why this structure
- Minimum effective dose: keep volume and intensity low so you rebuild without aggravating the Achilles. Two non-negotiable anchors this week are an easy aerobic run and a strength/mobility session. The short tempo provides a targeted stimulus without overreaching.
- Form and cadence focus to reduce Achilles stress: stay tall, keep cadence slightly higher than usual, and favor short, easy steps when in doubt.
This week (4-day layout you can shift to fit your schedule)
- Session A — Easy aerobic (35–40 minutes): 10–15 minute easy warm-up, 20–25 minutes easy running at conversational pace. Run relaxed, focus on cadence and tall posture. If any sharp or increasing Achilles pain appears, stop and switch to brisk walking or cross-train.
- Session B — Strength + mobility (25–35 minutes): short neuromuscular and calf-friendly strength session. Example: 2–3 rounds of single-leg heel raises 8–12 reps (slow up/slow down), glute bridges 10–12, single-leg RDLs 6–8 each side (light), plus 5–7 minutes calf mobility and ankle dorsiflexion work. No plyometrics; keep load controlled.
- Session C — Short tempo (30–40 minutes total): 10 minute warm-up, 3 × 4 minutes at comfortably hard (~close to planned half-marathon effort) with 2 minutes easy jog between, 8–10 minute cool-down. The tempo is deliberately short — keep it controlled and stop the quality early if Achilles twinges or if energy is low.
- Session D — Easy recovery or optional cross-train (30 minutes): very easy run, brisk walk, or bike. Keep effort easy and use this session for recovery and movement quality.
Adjustments and safety rules I’m applying this week
- Energy is the limiting signal: cut sessions short rather than forcing completion.
- If Achilles pain increases during any session, stop the run and do non-impact or walk; prioritize the strength/mobility work and recovery that day.
- If you feel better the next day, return to the plan cautiously rather than trying to make up missed work.
What I want you to report after sessions
Send a quick note after each session with: which session it was, duration, perceived effort (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. I’ll use those to adapt next week’s plan.
I’ll send the next plan weekly and adjust based on how these sessions land. Follow this week exactly as written and protect the anchors — that consistency is the fastest, safest way back to half-marathon fitness.

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Your mild Achilles tightness and Tuesday work mornings shape a cautious, steady start this week.
Here’s your four-session plan for the week, aligned with your four-session availability and built to protect your anchors while you rebuild for the May half-marathon.
Session 1 — Easy Aerobic (25–30 minutes)
- Pace: conversational, aim for RPE 3–5. Flat route if possible. Keep cadence light and posture tall. If Achilles twinges flare, back off and finish with a brief easy walk.
Session 2 — Mobility (15–20 minutes)
- Focus: Achilles and calf–ankle mobility plus hip openness. Include calf/soleus stretches, ankle mobilizations, hip circles, and light glides or step-throughs. Gentle and controlled throughout.
Session 3 — Easy Aerobic (25–30 minutes)
- Same guidelines as Session 1: easy pace, short and controlled, with attention to form and any Achilles signals. Use this to accumulate friendly cumulative load while staying under risk thresholds.
Session 4 — Strength (20–30 minutes)
- Lower-body and core with no impact work. Example moves: glute bridges, bodyweight squats or goblet squats if you have a light load, step-ups to a low step, calf raises, and a basic plank variation. Keep a controlled tempo, no plyometrics, and stop if pain rises. Three to four sets of 8–12 reps where appropriate.
Key coaching notes for the week
- Energy is the limiting signal, so today stays conservative. If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing it. If you rebound tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” the missed load.
- Keep the cadence light and posture tall on all runs. Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and downshifting overall stress to support recovery.
- Achilles safety: if pain increases during any session, switch to non-impact or walking and reassess before the next session.
What to report after each session (quick, concise): session name, duration, perceived effort (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. This keeps our plan precisely calibrated and minimizes risk as you rebuild.
This week is about establishing a safe, repeatable rhythm while protecting your Achilles and honoring your time. We’ll adapt next week based on how you respond and any updates from clinical guidance if available.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Your pain level is 2/10, and you’re available for four sessions this week on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday, so we’re sticking to a careful four-session base plan.
Week plan (base phase, four sessions):
- Monday: easy_aerobic run 25–30 minutes, comfortable pace, cadence light, posture tall.
- Wednesday: no-impact strength session 20–30 minutes, focusing on glutes, hips, and core with controlled loads; no plyometrics.
- Friday: easy_aerobic run 25–30 minutes, comfortable pace, cadence light.
- Sunday: tempo session 20–25 minutes at a controlled, sustainable hard pace, followed by a brief cooldown if needed.
Rationale: this four-session sequence supports a safe, gradual rebuild of running fitness while protecting the Achilles and honoring your available days. It keeps load predictable and aligned with your base phase goals.
Key execution notes:
- Stay conservative with load: easy runs around RPE 3–5/10; tempo around RPE 6–7/10. Do not push through pain.
- Achilles safety: stop if pain increases during a session, switch to non-impact or walking, and do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Cadence and posture: keep cadence light and posture tall during all runs and exercises.
- Recovery: prioritize sleep, nutrition, and simple recovery habits to support adaptation.
After each session, please report the session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. This helps us adjust quickly if needed.
If you need to shift a session by a few hours due to work, that’s fine—just keep the order and overall weekly load intact.
We’ll keep the plan flexible week to week, always prioritizing safety and gradual progress. Let me know how this week goes with your four sessions.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Your Achilles is holding at about 2/10, and yesterday’s 45-minute run was manageable with energy staying solid.
This week’s plan (4 sessions, conservative base work) aligns with Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun availability and your goal to rebuild safely for the May half marathon.
Monday — easy aerobic: 25–30 minutes at an easy effort (RPE 3–5). Keep cadence light and posture tall; stop if pain rises.
Wednesday — no-impact strength: 20–30 minutes focused on glutes, hips, and core; avoid plyometrics.
Friday — easy aerobic: 25–30 minutes at an easy effort (RPE 3–5).
Sunday — tempo: 20–25 minutes at a controlled, sustainable hard pace (RPE 6–7); finish with a brief cooldown.
Post-session reporting: after each session, send brief notes with session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. This helps us track response and adjust safely.
Safety and progression: If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and switch to non-impact walking. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week. You’ve already handled an outside run this week—keep today’s load in line with the plan and adjust if needed based on pain and sleep.
This plan keeps load steady, prioritizes sleep and recovery, and protects your Achilles while you rebuild fitness for the target race. — Coach

inbound.subject: Long run felt fine
inbound.body: Long run update: I got through 75 minutes this morning and honestly I feel ok after the long run. The Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and I slept well last night. I'd like to keep the same four-day rhythm this week if that still makes sense.

outbound.subject: Re: Long run felt fine
outbound.text:
75-minute run update: you reported the Achilles is in the background but not angry (2/10). You slept well, and you want to keep the four-day rhythm this week—that plan remains appropriate.
Here's your week, kept simple and controlled:
- Monday: Easy Aerobic 25–30 minutes at a comfortable pace (RPE 3–5). Cadence light, posture tall.
- Wednesday: No-impact Strength 20–30 minutes (glutes/hips/core; no plyometrics).
- Friday: Easy Aerobic 25–30 minutes (RPE 3–5).
- Sunday: Tempo 20–25 minutes at a controlled hard effort (RPE 6–7), with cooldown.
Safety reminders: If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and switch to non-impact or walking. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week. Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits.
Post-session reporting: after each session, please send session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. This keeps us aligned and lets us adjust quickly.
If anything changes (new clinician clearance, pain spike, or work schedule shifts), tell me right away so we can adapt.

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
For your comeback runs, easy should feel truly easy: you should be able to talk in full sentences without gasping. Aim for an RPE around 3–4 out of 10 on those easy days. If you feel especially good, you can edge toward a 5, but keep it comfortable and avoid chasing pace—the point is to protect mechanics and the Achilles.
Continue with your four-day rhythm (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun). With the Achilles at about 2/10 in the background, stay conservative: if pain moves toward 5/10 or becomes sharp, downgrade to non-impact or walking and don’t push through.
After each session, record session name, duration, RPE, Achilles pain, and last night’s sleep. If you have clinician clearance updates for running, let me know.

inbound.subject: Work got messy
inbound.body: Can we adjust the week? Work is crazy and I probably only have Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday for training. I missed one session already and my stress is definitely up.

outbound.subject: Re: Work got messy
outbound.text:
You said work is chaotic and you only have Wednesday, Friday and Sunday this week — that’s fine: we’ll protect consistency and the Achilles by shifting to a three-session, minimum‑effective dose week.
This week (Wed / Fri / Sun):
- Wednesday — Easy aerobic run (25–30 min total)
- Warm up 5 min easy walk/jog, then settle into conversational pace (RPE 3–4). Keep cadence smooth and relaxed; focus on short ground contact and upright posture.
- Monitor Achilles: if pain creeps toward 4–5/10 or becomes sharp, stop and switch to walking or bike; do not push to “make up” missed work.
- Friday — Strength / no‑impact session (20–25 min)
- Focus: glutes, hips, core; avoid jumps/plyometrics. Example structure: single‑leg glute bridges 2x8–10 each, split/step‑back lunges 2x6–8 each, Romanian‑style hip hinge or light deadlifts 2x8, side plank 2x25–40s each, dead‑bug 2x10. Add gentle calf isometrics (3x15s) only if Achilles pain is at baseline.
- Keep tempo controlled; prioritize technique over load.
- Sunday — Easy aerobic run (30–35 min total)
- Conversational pace (RPE 3–4). Keep effort smooth; don’t chase pace because you missed a session earlier in the week.
Key priorities and adjustments
- Energy is the limiting signal this week — stay conservative. If you feel flat, cut a session short rather than forcing completion. 
- Protect consistency: three controlled sessions this week are better than trying to cram in more. 
- Stay technically tidy (short ground contact, upright posture) to reduce Achilles load. 
- Focus on sleep, nutrition, and simple stress‑down tools (short breathing breaks, prioritize one evening for wind‑down).
If‑then safety rules (apply immediately)
- If Achilles pain increases toward ~5/10 or becomes sharp, stop running and switch to non‑impact (bike, swim, or walking) until pain settles; avoid heavy calf eccentrics or plyometrics while symptomatic. 
- If energy drops further, shorten the session or convert it to the strength/no‑impact option rather than pushing.
Reporting and next steps
- After each session, send a quick report: session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. I’ll use those to adjust next week.
Let me know how Wednesday goes and we’ll tweak Friday/Sunday based on how you feel.

inbound.subject: Here are the details you probably need
inbound.body: More detail for the adjustment: event date is 2026-05-17, days available this week are 3, pain score is still 2 out of 10, risk candidate feels yellow because work stress is high, week is chaotic true, stress score 8, sleep score 5.

outbound.subject: Re: Here are the details you probably need
outbound.text:
You’ve got Wednesday, Friday and Sunday available this week; Achilles pain 2/10, stress 8 and sleep 5 — we’ll run a conservative three-session week to protect the Achilles and keep load low.
This week’s plan (do not add harder work):
- Wed — Easy aerobic run, 25–30 minutes total. 5-minute walk/jog warm-up, then conversational pace (RPE 3–4). Keep cadence and form relaxed. If energy is low, shorten to 15–20 minutes or convert to a non-impact option (bike/elliptical).
- Fri — Strength / no-impact, 20–25 minutes. Focus on glutes, hips and core; avoid jumping or plyometrics. Keep tempo controlled and stop any exercise that irritates the Achilles.
- Sun — Easy aerobic run, 30–35 minutes total. Conversational pace (RPE 3–4). 5-minute easy cooldown. If the Achilles feels aggravated, switch to a non-impact session.
Execution priorities:
- Keep effort smooth and technically tidy rather than pushing duration or pace.
- If Achilles pain or overall fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easier or convert to non-impact — do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- If Achilles pain approaches ~5/10 or is sharp, stop running immediately and replace with non-impact. If pain is sharp or persistent, get clinical advice before returning to running.
Post-session reporting: after each session send — session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. That report helps me decide adjustments for next week.
Keep it simple this week: controlled efforts, short sessions, and prioritize sleep and easy recovery. Let me know how each session goes.

inbound.subject: Tune-up effort felt controlled
inbound.body: Small milestone: I did a tune-up 5k effort in 24:50 and it felt controlled. I added a 20m cool-down jog after. No big pain spike, just normal post-workout heaviness, and sleep was good.

outbound.subject: Re: Tune-up effort felt controlled
outbound.text:
Your tune-up 5k felt controlled, which is a solid sign given the busy week and the Achilles considerations. I’m keeping you on a light, three-session week that protects load while you rebuild base fitness for a half marathon.
Plan for Wed / Fri / Sun (anchor sessions):
- Wed — Easy aerobic run: 25–30 minutes total (5 minutes warm-up walk/jog, then conversational pace at RPE 3–4). If energy is very low, shorten to 15–20 minutes. Optional 5–10 minutes of mobility/recovery work after to support stiffness and sleep quality.
- Fri — Strength/no-impact: 20–25 minutes focusing on glutes, hips, and core; no plyometrics. Keep it light and controlled (e.g., glute bridges, side-lying leg lifts, dead bugs, hip lifts). No impact loading beyond these movements.
- Sun — Easy aerobic run: 30–35 minutes total (5 minutes easy cooldown at the end). Keep the pace smooth and conversation-friendly (RPE 3–4). If energy is low, shorten to 15–20 minutes or convert to non-impact activity (walking or cycling).
Key safety notes:
- If Achilles pain approaches 5/10 or becomes sharp, stop running and switch to non-impact activity immediately.
- Post-session reporting is essential: share session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep.
- If the week tightens, keep only the anchor sessions and downgrade any optional work.
Rationale: you’re juggling high stress and suboptimal sleep, so we keep the plan conservative but consistent. Two easy aerobic days and one short strength/mobility day protect the Achilles, support circulation, and preserve rhythm without overloading your system.
If you want a quick recap after each session, I’m happy to see your notes in a tight format (session name, duration, RPE, Achilles pain, sleep). Stay steady—you’re making progress with careful steps. Optional sign-off: let me know how the sessions feel after you’ve tried them.

inbound.subject: Still on the same four-day pattern
inbound.body: Just to confirm, I'm still doing four days per week most weeks. The same general Monday Wednesday Friday Sunday pattern still works, although Friday has become the easiest day for me to protect.

outbound.subject: Re: Still on the same four-day pattern
outbound.text:
You’re right—you can stick with four days per week, and we’ll keep Friday the lightest day to protect your Achilles.
Here’s the base-phase plan laid out for your four-day pattern (general moderate time), with a cautious progression and clear check-ins:
- Monday — easy aerobic: 25–30 minutes total at a conversational pace (RPE 3–4). Start with a 5-minute relaxed warm-up, run easy, and finish with a brief cooldown.
- Wednesday — strength: 20–25 minutes focusing on glutes, hips, and core (no plyometrics). Keep the effort light to moderate and the movements precise.
- Friday — easy aerobic: 25–30 minutes (RPE 3–4). If you feel good and energy is solid, you can stretch to 30–35 minutes, but keep it light and controlled.
- Sunday — skills: 15–25 minutes of form/technique work, with mobility as needed. This is about efficiency and mechanics rather than pace.
If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and don’t try to “make up” lost intensity later in the week. Safety first.
Post-session check-in: after each workout, please report session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. This helps keep your plan aligned with how you’re feeling and your current load.
Notes: clinical clearance for running remains unconfirmed, so we stay on the safe side with these conservative loads. The plan is designed to rebuild base fitness steadily while protecting the Achilles and keeping you on track for the May race.
If you want, I can tweak the Monday or Friday slightly based on how you feel mid-week, but we’ll keep Friday the lightest day as the default.
Let me know how it goes once you’ve tried a couple of sessions.

inbound.subject: Travel week coming up
inbound.body: Travel week heads-up: next week is rough. I only have Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday open, and hotel treadmill plus a tiny gym are all I'll have. I'd love the simplest travel-friendly version of the plan.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel week coming up
outbound.text:
Travel week is coming up, and you’ve got Tue, Thu, and Sat available with a hotel treadmill and a small gym. Here’s the simplest travel-friendly plan that protects your Achilles, keeps energy in check, and maintains momentum.
Tue – Easy Aerobic on treadmill, 25 minutes total
- 5-minute warm-up, then 15–20 minutes at a conversational pace (RPE 3–4), 0–1% incline, then 5-minute cooldown
- Keep effort light and relaxed; adjust pace to stay within that easy zone
- If energy is clearly lower, shorten to 20 minutes total
Thu – Strength, 20–25 minutes
- 2 rounds of: glute bridges 12 reps, dead bugs or bird dogs 10 per side, hip hinges with a chair 12 reps, side-lying or floor clam shells 12 per side (no resistance if you don’t have it)
- Rest 30–60 seconds between moves
- Focus on smooth, controlled movement; no plyometrics; keep impact minimal
Sat – Easy Aerobic on treadmill, 25–30 minutes
- Same structure as Tue: warm-up, 15–20 minutes easy, cooldown
- If energy is good, you may stay at 25–30 minutes; otherwise keep it toward the 25-minute end
Safety and adjustment notes
- If energy drops further, shorten the session rather than pushing through
- If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” missed work
- If Achilles pain approaches 5/10 or becomes sharp, stop running and switch to walking or a non-impact option
- If the gym/treadmill isn’t cooperative, substitute a brisk walk outside or a low-impact home alternative
Post-session check-in

outbound.html:
Travel week is coming up, and you’ve got Tue, Thu, and Sat available with a hotel treadmill and a small gym. Here’s the simplest travel-friendly plan that protects your Achilles, keeps energy in check, and maintains momentum.

Tue – Easy Aerobic on treadmill, 25 minutes total
- 5-minute warm-up, then 15–20 minutes at a conversational pace (RPE 3–4), 0–1% incline, then 5-minute cooldown
- Keep effort light and relaxed; adjust pace to stay within that easy zone
- If energy is clearly lower, shorten to 20 minutes total

Thu – Strength, 20–25 minutes
- 2 rounds of: glute bridges 12 reps, dead bugs or bird dogs 10 per side, hip hinges with a chair 12 reps, side-lying or floor clam shells 12 per side (no resistance if you don’t have it)
- Rest 30–60 seconds between moves
- Focus on smooth, controlled movement; no plyometrics; keep impact minimal

Sat – Easy Aerobic on treadmill, 25–30 minutes
- Same structure as Tue: warm-up, 15–20 minutes easy, cooldown
- If energy is good, you may stay at 25–30 minutes; otherwise keep it toward the 25-minute end

Safety and adjustment notes
- If energy drops further, shorten the session rather than pushing through
- If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” missed work
- If Achilles pain approaches 5/10 or becomes sharp, stop running and switch to walking or a non-impact option
- If the gym/treadmill isn’t cooperative, substitute a brisk walk outside or a low-impact home alternative

Post-session check-in
- After each session: record session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep

Normal cadence when you’re back home
- Friday remains the light day in your usual four-day pattern; this week is three focused sessions to stay healthy and consistent. Let’s use this as a template during travel and revert to the full pattern once you’re back in your usual environment.

Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Travel details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow because I am traveling for work. Week is chaotic true. Equipment access is treadmill and gym, no track. I can do 40m treadmill runs and one short strength session.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel details
outbound.text:
You’re traveling with a chaotic week and a mild Achilles bother (2/10) — we’ll keep everything controlled and simple. Three short, low-risk sessions that protect the tendon and keep you consistent: Tue (easy run), Thu (strength), Sat (easy + short skills).
Tuesday — Easy aerobic (treadmill)
- Total ~25 minutes: 5-min easy warm-up walk/jog, 15 minutes conversational running (RPE 3–4), 5-min cooldown. Use 0–1% incline. If energy and Achilles feel very good you may extend to 30–40 min, but only increase if pain stays <2/10 and effort stays easy; otherwise shorten to ~20 min.
Thursday — Strength (hotel gym, ~20–25 minutes)
- 2 rounds, controlled tempo, no plyometrics: glute bridges x12; dead-bug or bird-dog x10/side; hip-hinge/ Romanian-style single-leg or two-leg RDL x8–10; side-lying clams x12/side; standing double-leg calf raises x12 (slow). Rest 30–60s between sets. Focus on posture, hip control, and smooth reps — keep it easy on the Achilles.
Saturday — Easy aerobic + skills (treadmill, ~30 minutes total)
- 20–25 min easy treadmill run (same structure as Tue). Finish with 6–10 minutes of form/mobility: 2×30s single-leg balance each side, 2×20–30s easy marching or high-knee-in-place on treadmill (very low intensity), plus 2–3 calf/hip mobility drills and light foam roll.
Safety and execution rules (follow these exactly)
- If Achilles pain rises meaningfully (approaching ~5/10) or becomes sharp, stop running immediately and switch to walking or non-impact cross-training. Do not progress load that day.
- If energy or sleep is low, shorten the session rather than forcing full time. Do not make up missed intensity later in the week.
- No hard/interval sessions this week — keep efforts smooth and technically tidy.
What I need from you after each session
- Quick report: session name, duration, RPE (1–10), Achilles pain (0–10), and last night’s sleep. If you have any clinician updates about the Achilles, include that.
Keep sleep and simple recovery habits (hydration, short walking breaks between meetings) this week. Follow this exactly — it protects the tendon while keeping your fitness momentum. Let me know how the first session goes.
